sentences.text: Sonia Bielski, B-I-E-L-S-K-I.
Boldo, B-O-L-D-O.
I was born in Novogrudok, Poland.
I forgot already.
It makes me 74, 75, almost.
Meshulam Boldo, Meshulam.
He was born in Lubcz.
That's a small village.
That's not a city, a village, near Novogrudok, not far from Novogrudok.
Lida Baranowicze, and not far away from Vilna.
My mother's name was Pesia [?
Noshemovski. ?] No-she-movski.
She was born in a village, not far from Novogrudok, in Baranovichi.
Me?
I had four brothers.
Yeah.
My oldest brother was Mordechai.
Second was Itzchak.
The third I was.
And then was Zalman, and Yankele.
No.
My grandparents died in Israel about 10 years ago.
They were together with us in the war.
And after the war, they wanted to go to Israel.
So my husband bought an apartment in Israel, and they went to Israel.
Yes.
In the war.
Not grandparents, no.
My mother and father.
I didn't have grandparents already.
My grandparents, they die in Poland, not from the Germans, by themselves, from old age.
Oh, sure, I remember.
My mother's father was Rabbi Hirsh.
When he died, he was 105 years old, a beautiful, beautiful old man.
And the people around him, they were coming, he should bless them always.
And my grandma was probably about 98 years.
She lived after he died.
She never knew that he died.
But we had a woman and a man.
They were taking care on them.
Oh, yeah.
They lived not far from us, about two streets from us.
My mother was the youngest child there.
So she was always there.
When we went to school, something, she was coming, and see if everything is taken care.
And we always came Sunday or Saturday, or when there was a holiday.
We saw always grandpa in the synagogue.
He had a synagogue not far from the house where he lived.
Oh, he was a rabbi.
Yes.
Yeah.
My father had a father, Shmuel.
But he died very young.
I remember when he died.
He was about 50 or 60 years old.
I remember the night when he died.
And his mother was not this mother, but was the second mother.
But she was so good to him, that we called her grandma.
I couldn't-- her name was-- wait a second.
I put in on the paper.
Now I don't remember already.
Whatever.
But she was wonderful.
She was so good when my grandpa died.
We was holding her like she was really a grandma.
No, no.
We had a big house.
And this was three apartments.
This was apartment special for them, but in the same house.
Yeah, we had a big house on [?
Tertmay ?], in Novogrudok.
We had about six rooms, and a big kitchen, and a big dining room.
I had-- my brothers, they were together in two rooms.
I had my room myself.
My mother had a big room, father and mother's.
And all the parties, they were made in my mother's room.
This was like a ballroom.
I had too a very beautiful room.
My room was separate from my brothers, a little further.
And the walls were covered with Persian rugs.
This was two big Persian rugs on the walls and one on the floor.
And I have a big couch, a big bed.
This was like a couch, not a bed.
And I had my table, because I work in there.
When the war began, I have here friends of mine.
She was not my friend, she was friends of my brothers, Zalman, Sula Wolozynska.
She-- her house was demolished.
She lived with the father and mother.
They had-- her father was a dentist, and the mother was a dentist.
And she had another sister.
One sister was Sula.
And the other was Rita.
Wait a second.
The house was demolished.
They couldn't find a place.
So my mother said to her mother, you know what?
Go ask Sonia if she is ready to give to you her room.
If Sonia will give you her room, I will gladly accept you to my house.
OK?
And her father and mother came to us.
And we were sitting quietly.
Because this was already, the German was around and fighting, and throwing the bombs from all over.
So we're sitting quietly.
And they opened the door, and they come in.
And they said, Sonia, your mother said, if you can give me a room.
I said, why should I give you my room?
She said, because I don't have where to go to sleep.
I said, gladly, I will give you my room.
And they were six weeks in my room, four of them.
And she, when you will have a chance, she will describe you my room.
It was Persian rugs on the walls and a big Persian rug on the floor.
And my couch was for two people.
So my mother and father put them another bed, two bed.
And they lived in my room for six weeks.
And they ate with us.
And they lived with us.
What?
I stayed with my mother in her room, with my mother and father.
We had a dining room, a big dining room.
When we came from school, about two o'clock, not later, we had supper.
Supper was not at night like here.
But supper was between one and two o'clock.
We had a woman, she was working 14 or 15 years, an old [INAUDIBLE].
A woman from the village.
And she was sleeping by us, and working.
And she was cooking.
And we had another cousin of my mother's, what she didn't have nobody in the whole world.
And my father went, before the war, he went and he brought her in to our house.
And she stayed with us, and lived with us, till the end of her life.
So and I slept with my mother and father in the room.
We had another two rooms, small ones.
This was for the servant.
And one was for Eshke, my mother's cousin.
You remember the servant's name?
I forgot.
Aneczka!
Aneczka.
You see, when I talking, you remember.
And I remember like that, that about two years, three years before the war broke up, my mother send you to United States.
Because she had a son in the United States.
Aneczka.
The woman, who she was working for us, from the village, she had a son.
And her husband went to United States.
And he took away her son from her.
But after a couple of years, he began to write to us that he would like the mother should come to United States.
So my mother took her to Warsaw.
She made a papers for her.
And she send her off to United States.
What were the religious practices of the home?
My mother was very religious, orthodox.
My father, I cannot say that he was very orthodox, but he loved her very much.
And he did everything what my mother wanted.
So he was too.
He went to the synagogue.
He was a rabbi in the synagogue.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he observed all the Jewish things, what he had to.
The holiday in the house was something to remember.
We never ate the suppers for ourselves.
We had always people [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH]?
This is people what they didn't have the families.
They were studying Torah, in the city.
And they didn't have where to eat.
My mother and father, they were holding 19 boys.
They were eating in time from the week, 19 boys.
They were changing.
They were eating by us.
I will tell you another story.
My husband had a brother, a rabbi.
And he too lived in Novogrudok.
They were not too rich.
It was a big family.
And he was eating by us days, the rabbi.
His day was Monday, or Tuesday, right?
So this was divided.
The whole week was divided between the 19 boys, yes?
And my mother took in the Bielski [INAUDIBLE].
He was a rabbi.
And he was Zusia's brother.
They were not religious.
But this one was very religious.
Zusia.
Zusia [INAUDIBLE].
And he was eating by us.
Zusia was my husband.
And Tuvia.
Yeah.
And sure, later.
And Tuvia's, and Asael's and [?
Itzchak's. ?] And he was starting in Novogrudok.
Probably didn't have enough for the food.
So he was asking where can he go to eat, because he was very kosher.
So they said, you know what?
You ask the Boldos.
There is kosher food.
So we took him every week.
One day of the week he was eating by us.
So how did a dinner like that look?
The dinner was-- you see, my father-- we had two [NON-ENGLISH].
This is farms.
One farm was ours.
And the other farm was [NON-ENGLISH].
From the Polack.
And my father was making cheeses, like from Holland, you have the round cheeses.
And they were coming from Vilna, from Warsaw with the trucks to take away the cheeses.
Right?
Now, he was buying meat for the Polish army.
So we had everything.
We had meat, there were cheeses, and butter, and everything.
There was not a thing that we didn't have it.
Was that your father's business?
That's my father's business was, right.
Cheese, and meat.
And we had the chickens and [NON-ENGLISH].
No, no, no.
We didn't sell it.
No.
The meat was send off to the Polish army.
The cheeses were sent off to the big stores in Vilna and Warsaw.
But there is enough was for us too.
You understand?
We had enough, everything.
We had two kibbutzim for [NON-ENGLISH] in Novogrudok.
And my father and mother, they gave to them two houses, from [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were sending them bread, and butter, and cheeses.
My whole family brothers, they were [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
We had about three houses.
So when the people moved out from there, my brother, [INAUDIBLE], said, Papi, we need the house for the kibbutz.
They're coming from all over the [NON-ENGLISH].
We need a house.
He said, OK.
You know what?
Take the house and make a kibbutz.
And that was a kibbutz.
This was across our house.
And when come Monday, or Thursday when [NON-ENGLISH], you know, when everybody was coming to the market, they were sending him cheeses, and butter, and whatever they needed, and bread, everything.
Oh, my mother loved movies, and loved reading.
She was reading a lot.
She was a [NON-ENGLISH].
She was working very hard.
She wanted the children to know everything.
We had tutors in the house, Hebrew tutors.
I remember that a rabbi was coming and was studying with the boys [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it is?
[NON-ENGLISH].
I remember now.
Because they were [NON-ENGLISH].
They were not very religious.
So my mother said, OK.
You're not religious, but I want you to know about the religion because I'm a Jew and you are a Jew too.
So they gave it to them everything in a house.
You understand?
So they knew everything.
They're studying everything.
That's it.
And full with friends.
Full with friends.
The house was full with friends always.
Saturday night, were about 10, 15, people eating by us.
Saturday night was like this written in the calendar.
They were eating potatoes, herring, cream.
Everything [NON-ENGLISH], not meat.
Dairy.
And for sitting about 15, 12 people.
And they were talking about the politics, and about Israel, and everything.
This was it.
I was not always there, the truth because I didn't believe in this.
I went to a Polish [NON-ENGLISH], a Polish school, where only 2%, 3% Jewish girls and boys went.
So I was far away from them.
I never belonged to [NON-ENGLISH].
I far away from this [NON-ENGLISH].
You see, my brothers were in the [NON-ENGLISH].
They were working there.
And they took in many boys and girls, but they didn't have it how to study.
So there, in the [NON-ENGLISH], they gave it to him.
This for study with them.
They were learning about Israel.
They're learning about [NON-ENGLISH] Israel, and everything.
What language was spoken at home?
Yiddish.
But my brothers were very fluently in Hebrew.
That's it.
Me?
I was attending high school, from the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means from the-- [NON-ENGLISH] mean from the-- from the Polish state.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was attending there the high school.
And after the high school, I attended [NON-ENGLISH], like two years college here.
This was French, history, Polish, and Latin.
And after that, I did the two years.
My mother and father wanted me to go to Sorbonne, for dentistry.
Because I had a very big family in Paris, and very rich.
And they said, let's send Sonia here.
And we'll give her the qualification what she wanted.
And in '37, 1937, my mother took me to Paris to see the family, if I like it, to go there.
For a while I liked it, but I was very miserable.
I didn't know how to talk French.
I was miserable.
I studied in school, but this was not enough.
So when I came after that, my mommy took me, a madame.
A madame, a teacher, a French, where she was coming two hours a day.
And she was conversation with me in French.
When the war began, I was better speaking French than I'm speaking now English.
But with the time, even you're not speaking a language, you forgetting.
I'm reading.
And I'm writing.
What?
I had big ambitions.
First of all, I wanted to go from Novogrudok.
A time from Noel, that's [NON-ENGLISH].
That's New Year, the Polish, yes?
My father was taking me always to Vilna or to Warsaw.
They were buying me everything what I were thinking about it, a jewelry, shoes.
They were not buying me in the stores, but they were making orders for me.
I was only 15, 16.
And they were making already orders [NON-ENGLISH], Vilna.
This was the biggest stores for the making the shoes.
They made shoes for me there.
They made a fur coat for me.
I have a jacket for me, from mink.
I wanted to go to see the world.
When I came in '37, we were there three weeks in Paris.
I saw the world there.
I didn't see that in Novogrudok.
I saw Novogrudok as a village then.
Till I went out from Novogrudok, I was thinking that's a big city.
But when I came, I said to my mother, you know what?
This is not-- it's a village.
She said, my [INAUDIBLE].
Because you saw Paris.
What kind of a neighborhood did you live in Novogrudok?
Who were your neighbors?
A doctor, and a church.
We have about two Jewish families.
And the rest of them, they were Polish.
And they were very good.
We were very friendly with them.
My father was working, and they give it to them everything, everything.
Somebody was knocking at the door, and my father and mother ask them, what do you need?
We need something, whatever.
My father and mother went there right away.
They give it to them.
In the time before the war, there were Polish people what they were knocking at the door, and the windows, all over.
To us, they didn't knock.
They were telling to my father, please, close the windows and everything.
And don't go out in this and this hours outside.
Yes.
No.
Novogrudok was a town, mostly they were a Jewish town.
Mostly they were Jewish people.
Later, in the time when they built the big things, after Mickiewicz.
This is a romantic, a Polish romantic.
I think everybody know in the literature who is Mickiewicz was.
Adam Mickiewicz.
He was a big writer, a Polish.
So when they began to build in his name many things.
They got [NON-ENGLISH].
What is [NON-ENGLISH]?
All Pole.
Poland was divided now [INAUDIBLE].
The biggest cities was [INAUDIBLE].
The smaller [INAUDIBLE] was not the [INAUDIBLE].
Because Mickiewicz was born in Novogrudok, they gave it to Novogrudok the [INAUDIBLE].
So then the Polish people began to come in to Novogrudok.
They begin big offices.
How big a population did Novogrudok have?
I think about 15,000, 18,000 people, no more.
But this was enough.
I think about 8,000.
You see, our city had many Jews, and they were very rich.
I had a friend, Zaria, which she's now in Israel.
They had [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
From the tree they were making for building the [NON-ENGLISH].
Lumber yard, yes.
Lumber yard.
And they were very rich.
They were about four brothers.
And they had children.
And they were very, very, rich.
There was another family, [?
Broido. ?] They were too very, very rich.
They were making from the corn, they were making flours.
And they were giving a lot to the Polish [NON-ENGLISH], I mean to the Polish-- for the-- like you see in our organization, yes?
So for the Polish people.
So they didn't fight the Jew, the big ones.
But we had many Jewish people what they were very, very poor.
So we had for them, the old people, we had places for where they took the old people there.
And everybody was giving them bread, and everything.
They called this like old age, old ages.
And the rich one, they were holding onto them.
They giving them everything.
But mostly, we cannot talk about this 5%, or the 3% they were very rich.
Rest of them, they were very, very poor.
I don't know how they lived.
They didn't die from hunger.
No.
I think they were working on shuls.
They were working on dresses.
And many, many didn't work at all.
Yes.
Yes, was movies.
Three movies we had in Novogrudok.
Three movies.
We had the theater who once in a month, they were coming.
Not Jewish.
This come from Warsaw.
It was coming Polish.
It was some life.
But I didn't belong there.
Because this was in Jewish language.
Probably, yes.
But I was not into the library.
I went to the Polish library.
Because in our school, where there were the 3% Jewish people, they were asking us not to talk Yiddish, because of the language.
We were talking the language.
This was like a [INAUDIBLE].
It's not a clean Polish.
And they were making that you have to improve your language if you want to go to school.
You understand?
So there were reading enough Polish books.
We had libraries in the gymnasium.
And we had for this week, we had to finish two books.
What would you say the relationship, generally, of the Jewish population was with the non-Jewish population?
Not so good.
Not so good.
I will tell you.
I know the non-Jewish, they hate us.
The Polish people, they hate us, the Jewish.
They were saying, heh, [NON-ENGLISH].
You came for [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
You came to beg, to beg something.
What do you want to beg?
You want money?
Go to Israel.
You don't have to beg us for something.
There was [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is a [NON-ENGLISH]?
[NON-ENGLISH] is now you have Blue Cross and Blue Shield.
In Poland you were not paying for that.
You had.
So when they came, and this was a couple of Jew waiting, the Polish was the first.
Always the first.
The Jew was always the last one.
We have to break now.
